food_name: Com Tam (Vietnamese Broken Rice Plate with Grilled Pork Chop, Steamed Egg Meatloaf, and Shredded Pork Skin)
cuisine: Vietnamese (Southern — Ho Chi Minh City / Saigon)
food_subgroup: Vietnamese broken rice plate with grilled pork chop (sườn), steamed egg meatloaf (chả trứng), and shredded pork skin (bì), served with nuoc cham dipping sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Com Tam (Vietnamese broken rice plate). FOODON_03315674 (rice plate) is used as the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vietnamese broken rice plate (Com Tam) — closest parent match in FoodEx2; no exact code exists for this composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CMF is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a mixed rice plate. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Com Tam specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Com Tam or Vietnamese broken rice plate as of 2026.

preparation_method: grilled (pork chop — charcoal or gas grill)
mixed dipping sauce (nuoc cham — fish sauce, lime, sugar, water, chilli)
main_ingredients: nuoc cham dipping sauce (nước chấm — fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, water, garlic, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Com Tam plate weighs approximately 500g, comprising 200g cooked broken rice, approximately 200–250g of mixed protein toppings (grilled pork chop, steamed egg meatloaf, shredded pork skin), and approximately 50ml nuoc cham dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (SEA) — 2020 Edition; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual components (cooked white rice, pork loin grilled, egg)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Com Tam as a composite dish is not listed as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020. Per-100g values are derived from the FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 table for cooked broken rice and pork-based mixed rice dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for grilled pork loin (NDB 10219), cooked white rice (NDB 20444), and whole egg steamed preparations. The composite per-100g profile reflects the weighted average of the full plate (500g) as described in the input record. Values are consistent with the input record's verified data and are accepted as adapted estimates from approved sources.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); University of Sydney GI Database — white rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Com Tam (Vietnamese broken rice plate) as a composite dish. GI of 72 is derived from the analogous food 'white rice, cooked' (GI ~70–74, mean ~72) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Broken rice (cơm tấm) is a fragmented form of jasmine white rice; grain fragmentation does not alter starch crystallinity or GI — the GI of broken rice is equivalent to whole white rice. GL per plate = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 65) / 100 = 46.8. Carbohydrate per 500g plate = 65g (13.0g per 100g × 5).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,050mg per plate (500g), representing 52.5% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2,000mg
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should be specifically asked about nuoc cham use, as the fish-sauce-based dipping sauce contributes approximately 500mg sodium per serving (nearly half the plate's total sodium) and is frequently omitted from dietary recall; reducing nuoc cham volume from 50ml to 20ml or diluting with additional water and lime juice can reduce sodium intake by 200–300mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load — GL 46.8 per plate, driven by 65g total carbohydrate from broken rice (GI 72), pork marinade sugars, and nuoc cham sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should be counselled that one standard Com Tam plate produces a glycemic load equivalent to approximately three bowls of white rice; portion reduction of the broken rice component to 100g (from 200g) and requesting nuoc cham on the side (to limit added sugar) are the two highest-impact modifications for postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.6g per 100g; ~8g per plate) combined with high sodium (1,050mg per plate) and charcoal-grilling-derived heterocyclic amines (HCAs) and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) from the pork chop
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Com Tam frequency to occasional consumption; requesting the pork chop steamed or pan-fried rather than charcoal-grilled reduces HCA and PAH exposure, and requesting nuoc cham on the side or substituting with lime juice reduces both sodium and added sugar load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Charcoal-grilled pork chop generates pro-inflammatory HCAs and PAHs; high nuoc cham sodium may exacerbate corticosteroid-related fluid retention
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis on corticosteroid therapy should be advised to limit nuoc cham use to reduce sodium-driven fluid retention, and to prefer steamed preparations over charcoal-grilled pork to minimise HCA and PAH exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Com Tam (Cơm Tấm) is the iconic broken rice plate of Southern Vietnam, originating in Ho Chi Minh City (Saigon) and eaten for breakfast, lunch, and dinner by millions of Vietnamese daily. 'Tấm' refers to the small fragmented grains of jasmine rice produced during milling — historically a cheap by-product sold to workers, now the preferred rice texture for its slightly porous, rougher surface that absorbs nuoc cham sauce more effectively than whole-grain rice. A standard plate (~500g) comprises: (1) approximately 200g cooked broken jasmine rice; (2) a charcoal-grilled pork chop (sườn heo) marinated in fish sauce, sugar, lemongrass, and garlic; (3) steamed egg meatloaf (chả trứng hấp) — a savoury custard-like loaf of minced pork, egg, and glass noodles; (4) shredded pork skin (bì) tossed with toasted rice powder; and (5) approximately 50ml nuoc cham dipping sauce (nước chấm — fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, water, garlic, chilli). Per plate: approximately 640 kcal, 30g protein, 22g fat, 65g carbohydrate, GI ~72 (high), GL ~46.8 (very high), ~1,050mg sodium. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTES: (1) The broken rice GI is identical to whole white rice (~72) — grain fragmentation does not alter starch structure or glycaemic response. (2) The nuoc cham dipping sauce contributes approximately 500mg sodium per plate — nearly half the total sodium — and is frequently omitted from patient dietary recall. (3) The glycemic load of 46.8 per plate is equivalent to approximately three bowls of standard white rice. Available at Vietnamese restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.
common_user_queries: broken rice vs white rice GI

bi: Vietnamese pork skin with toasted rice powder

confidence_reason: Com Tam as a composite dish is not listed as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA FoodData Central. Per-100g values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 mixed rice dish entries and USDA SR Legacy component data for grilled pork loin, cooked white rice, and steamed egg preparations. The input record's verified values are consistent with these approved sources and are accepted as a medium-confidence adapted estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (white rice, GI 72). Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source entry exactly matches the full Com Tam composite plate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the Com Tam composite plate (broken rice + grilled pork chop + steamed egg meatloaf + shredded pork skin + nuoc cham) is not available as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite plate is not reported in the available approved sources. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace for this preparation based on FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 and USDA SR Legacy pork and rice entries; no industrial hydrogenation is involved. All other per_100g values are numeric and sourced from approved databases. The input record's FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 citation is retained as the primary source anchor; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is used as a secondary cross-reference for component-level validation. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (white rice); no direct GI measurement for Com Tam as a composite dish exists in the approved GI literature. GL per plate = 46.8 (72 × 65g carbohydrate per 500g plate / 100). Nuoc cham is included in the 500g plate weight and sodium calculation. The dish is not halal-certified as standard preparations use pork.
